Q1: Honestly, my study routine has been a constant negotiation with everything else in my life. I'm 27, so I have responsibilities that younger students might not have, and with limited resources, I can't always afford to just sit and study for hours. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after work and on weekends, usually listening to lectures while doing other tasks since I learn best by hearing things. I aimed for about 27 hours a week, but some weeks that was spread thin across six online courses. It wasn't elegant, but I made it work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was happening in my personal life. I wanted to be consistent, but with high stress levels and anxiety, some weeks I just couldn't stick to a schedule. When assignments were due, I'd study intensely for days straight. Other times, when I felt overwhelmed, I might not touch anything for a few days and then have to catch up. The unpredictability of my life made regular times almost impossible to maintain.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or needing to check if there were new announcements or feedback on my work. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was staying connected to the course, even if I didn't do much once I was there. The anxiety of missing something important was a big motivator too. I'd get nervous that I'd missed a deadline or an important update, so I'd log in to ease that worry.
Q4: Yes, eventually checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. After the first few weeks, I got into the habit of logging in during my lunch break and again in the evening. It became part of my daily routine because I was managing six courses and couldn't afford to fall behind. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me build these habits by working through my anxiety about missing things.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over 180 minutes of video content because hearing explanations really helps things click for me. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers made me feel less isolated in my struggles. When someone else asked a question I had, it normalized my confusion and helped me understand the material better.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the reading materials, especially long PDFs, because they just don't work well for how I learn. I'd skim them at best or ignore them entirely if there was a video covering the same content. Some of the optional supplementary resources I never touched because I was already stretched thin managing six courses with limited time and energy. I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. I also valued activities where I could hear explanations rather than just read them. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly tie to my success in the course, I'd deprioritize it. With my anxiety levels, I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent wisely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had my attention because they affected my grade, but optional ones often got pushed aside when stress was high. There were moments when I wanted to explore optional content, but my anxiety would kick in reminding me I hadn't finished the required stuff yet. It's frustrating because I'm genuinely curious, but survival mode doesn't leave room for exploration.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen while commuting or doing chores since I don't always have reliable internet access at home. I took handwritten notes while listening and reviewed those instead of going back to the LMS. I also used free resources from YouTube and podcasts to supplement what I was learning. My LMS activity doesn't capture probably half of my actual study time.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or listening to downloaded lectures on my phone without any internet connection. I'd also spend time just thinking through concepts while doing other tasks, which is a valid form of learning but leaves no digital trace. Sometimes I'd discuss course content with a friend who was taking similar classes, and that helped me process information. None of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio if possible, and then study from that offline for days or weeks. With limited internet access and resources, I couldn't afford to stream videos repeatedly. I'd take detailed notes during that first viewing and then work from those notes exclusively. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content dozens of times offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging with anything felt impossible. Around midterms, I was managing deadlines across six courses simultaneously, and I just shut down for a few days. Financial stress also crept in, making it hard to focus on academics when I was worried about basic needs. The CBT sessions helped me develop coping strategies, but there were still dark moments when the course felt insurmountable.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy because I was always tired from juggling so much. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other essential tasks. The confusion from managing six different courses also meant I sometimes mixed up deadlines or requirements, which added to my stress.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. There were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about money or other problems. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. My anxiety made it hard to be present sometimes, even when I was technically engaged with the materials. Those sessions felt wasted, but I didn't know how else to cope.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My anxiety made starting things feel overwhelming, so I'd avoid them until the deadline forced my hand. There was also a fear of failure that made me delay, like if I didn't start, I couldn't fail yet. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still a daily struggle. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, watching videos and posting in discussions. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift and lose focus entirely given everything else competing for my attention. They created necessary pressure, but also spiked my anxiety significantly. My engagement pattern was very much deadline-driven rather than steady.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on my motivation. When I got positive feedback, it gave me hope and energy to keep going despite everything. Negative or critical feedback sometimes sent me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course for a few days. I learned to wait until I was in a better headspace to read feedback so I could process it constructively. The timing of when I checked feedback became strategic for my mental health.
Q18: The course structure both helped and hindered me. Clear deadlines and organized modules made it easier to plan my limited study time. However, the volume of content across my six courses sometimes felt impossible to manage. I appreciated when instructors broke things into smaller chunks because that felt more achievable. The structure worked best when it acknowledged that students have lives outside the course.
Q19: Having video content available was essential for my auditory learning style, so that made engagement much easier. The discussion forums being active also helped me feel connected despite studying mostly alone. What made things harder was when important information was buried in long text documents I didn't have time to read thoroughly. Also, when deadlines clustered together across courses, the design felt punishing rather than supportive.
Q20: I think that could reflect students who are trying hard but struggling, like me with my anxiety and stress. You might log in repeatedly, watch videos, read discussions, but still not be able to complete assignments because of mental health barriers or life circumstances. High activity could show genuine effort and engagement with learning, even if the final submissions don't materialize. It's not laziness; it's often invisible struggles.
Q21: That definitely reflects part of my experience. I learned a lot offline through downloaded materials and personal notes, so my LMS activity was lower than my actual engagement. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need to spend hours on the platform to understand the material. My exam score was decent despite my lower task completion because I understood concepts even if I couldn't always submit work. The LMS only captures one slice of learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and submission timestamps. They capture patterns like deadline-driven behavior pretty well. But they completely miss offline studying, mental engagement versus passive scrolling, and the quality of learning happening. They can't measure whether I actually understood something or just watched a video while stressed and distracted. The emotional and cognitive dimensions are invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, even when it's hard. For me, it means showing up despite my anxiety, asking questions when I'm confused, and applying what I learn. It's not just about completing tasks but about caring enough to struggle through difficulties. Engagement is mental and emotional investment, not just clicking through content. Sometimes my most engaged moments left no trace in any system.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how hard I fought my anxiety to even open my laptop some days. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing dishes or commuting. It can't measure the growth I experienced through CBT sessions that helped me become a better student. My resilience, my offline study habits, my peer discussions outside the platform, my genuine love of learning despite limited resources, none of that appears in the data. I'm more than my click patterns.
